agent1_snippets.title: Primary Ovarian Insufficiency -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: The physical exam should include vital signs including blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, and weight/body mass index. Then, a general survey of the patient should be performed looking for any pathognomonic signs of disease. For example, hirsutism, obesity, and acne may be indicative of PCOS or short stature, increased carrying angle, broad chest, and webbing of the neck may be indicative of Turner Syndrome. [3] [10] [11] The physical examination should also include palpation of the thyroid gland, which may indicate thyroid disease, and pelvic examination, which can help to identify any visible abnormalities of the genitourinary system.

agent1_snippets.title: Amenorrhea -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination
agent1_snippets.content: Skin and Hair : Adolescent patients with primary amenorrhea should be examined for the presence and maturation of axillary and pubic hair; their presence indicates exposure to androgens, most likely from functional ovaries. All patients should be examined for signs of hyperandrogenism, including male-pattern hair growth, hair loss, and acne. Thyroid disorders may also present with skin, hair, and nail changes, while patients with PCOS or uncontrolled diabetes mellitus may develop acanthosis nigricans. Clinicians should be aware that many women remove undesired male-pattern hair growth, so it may not be present on physical exam; asking about any hair removal practices (eg, shaving, waxing, laser) is essential. [18]

agent1_snippets.title: Amenorrhea -- Treatment / Management -- Other Endocrine Abnormalities Affecting the HPO Axis
agent1_snippets.content: Similarly, patients with other endocrine abnormalities resulting in amenorrhea should be managed with the improvement of the underlying etiology as the primary goal. For instance, lifestyle interventions promoting a healthy diet and exercise program are the most beneficial therapy for some conditions (eg, PCOS, diabetes). In contrast, others may require pharmacological treatment (eg, thyroid disorders) or a combination. [7] Some medicines also aim to treat complications that may arise due to these conditions. In patients with chronic anovulatory cycles (eg, PCOS), combined hormonal contraceptives are first-line therapy to prevent the development of endometrial hyperplasia and malignancy and treat hirsutism and acne. [18] Clinicians should also perform preventive management and screen patients for hypertension and an elevated body mass index at each visit. Furthermore, patients should be screened for dyslipidemia and impaired glucose tolerance (eg, 2-hour oral glucose tolerance testing or hemoglobin A1C level) every three to five years. [7]

agent1_snippets.title: Glucose Intolerance -- Evaluation -- Screening
agent1_snippets.content: It is estimated that the onset of DM occurs 4 to 7 years before its clinical diagnosis. Screening for hyperglycemia can identify individuals who are at risk for preventable diabetes complications. Persons more than 40 years of age should be screened annually. More frequent screening is recommended in individuals with additional risk factors for diabetes. These include: Certain races/ethnicities (Native American, African American, Hispanics or Asian American, Pacific Islander) Overweight or obese persons: BMI greater than or equal to 25 kg/m2 or 23 kg/m2 in Asian Americans First-degree relative with diabetes History of CVD, hypertension, low HDL-cholesterol or hypertriglyceridemia Women with polycystic ovarian syndrome Physical inactivity Conditions associated with insulin resistance such as acanthosis nigricans

agent1_snippets.title: Prediabetes -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: In majority of the patients with prediabets do not experience any symptoms and hence appropriate screening and monitoring especially in individuals with family history is needed. In the minority of patients who do experience symptoms, they can be as follows: Increased appetite Unexplained weight loss/weight gain High BMI Weakness Fatigue Sweating Blurred vision Slow healing cuts or bruises Recurrent skin infections/gum bleeding The single sign of prediabetes is elevated blood glucose on a blood test that is not high enough to be classified as type 2 diabetes mellitus.

agent1_snippets.title: Primary Amenorrhea -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination
agent1_snippets.content: Skin and hair : The presence and maturation of sexual hair (axillary and pubic hair), which indicates exposure to androgens, should be assessed. Note that the absence of sexual hair suggests the patient has never been exposed to androgens. Signs of androgen excess include hirsutism, excessive acne, and virilization (ie, enlarged clitoris, deepening of the voice). Multiple pigmented naevi may be seen with Turner syndrome. Patients with PCOS or uncontrolled diabetes mellitus may develop acanthosis nigricans. Skin, hair, and nail changes can also be seen with thyroid disorders. Clinicians should be aware that many women remove undesired male-pattern hair growth, so it may not be present on physical exam; it is essential to inquire about any hair removal practices (eg, shaving, waxing, laser). [19]

Pay particular attention to the teeth, noting tooth decay from repeated vomiting and subsequent hard brushing, and parotid glands for evidence of hypertrophy, as with bulimia nervosa. Perform visual field testing to assess for pituitary macroadenoma; if large enough, it may press on the optic chiasm and cause bitemporal non-homonomous hemianopsia. A thorough skin exam should also be performed. A pelvic examination should be performed when appropriate, particularly when a patient presents with delayed or disordered menarche.

agent1_snippets.title: Secondary Amenorrhea -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: Polycystic ovarian syndrome and other hyperandrogenic disorders : Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) is the most common cause of hyperandrogenism associated with secondary amenorrhea and is characterized by a combination of menstrual abnormalities, hyperandrogenism, and metabolic dysfunction. [3] [20] These patients will frequently have a longer history of irregular cycles before their current period of amenorrhea, along with evidence of hirsutism (ie, male pattern hair growth), acne, obesity, or insulin resistance. Non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia, androgenic tumors, and Cushing syndrome can have similar presentations to PCOS, though androgenic tumors are often associated with much higher levels of abnormal androgen production, potentially leading to virilization (eg, clitoromegaly, deepening of the voice) that is not typically seen in PCOS. Additionally, patients with Cushing syndrome often have other associated symptoms and clinical findings. All patients with secondary amenorrhea should be examined for signs of hyperandrogenism (eg, hirsutism, acne, and virilization) and asked about undesired hair growth that may have already been removed (eg, shaving, waxing, laser).

agent1_snippets.title: Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
agent1_snippets.content: PCOS is a diagnosis encompassing several symptoms and abnormalities that affect multiple parts of the body. PCOS is primarily a condition of increased male hormone activity, which can manifest through symptoms like male-pattern hair growth (hirsutism), scalp hair loss, and acne, or be detected through hormone blood tests. Additional symptoms may include irregular cycles and infertility. Diagnosis is made by thoroughly assessing symptoms, performing physical exams, conducting laboratory tests, and utilizing imaging techniques. No single test can confirm or exclude the diagnosis of PCOS.

agent1_snippets.content: Childhood obesity quadruples the risk of developing glucose intolerance and non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (NIDDM or Type 2 diabetes). Over 85% of children with NIDDM are either overweight or obese at diagnosis. [23] Acanthosis nigricans is an increased pigmentation and thickness of the skin in intertriginous folds, and it is usually associated with glucose intolerance in children and adolescents. Fasting insulin and glucose should be included in the evaluation of childhood obesity. The risk factors for type 2 non-insulin-dependent diabetes and metabolic syndrome include, children with BMI 85th to 95th percentile along with, immediate family history of type 2 diabetes signs of insulin resistance such as acanthosis nigricans, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and polycystic ovarian syndrome. Children with BMI >95th percentile regardless of family history or associated features. [24]

agent1_snippets.title: Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
agent1_snippets.content: PCOS can run in the family, where not just 1 gene, but the interaction of multiple genes might be responsible for this condition. PCOS is a common medical condition, and it is estimated that in certain ethnicities, it can be present in 1 in 4 women between the ages of 18 and 35 years old. An abnormality in the metabolism and storage of energy, known as insulin resistance, which is also the root cause of diabetes and obesity, has been implicated in causing and perpetuating this abnormal hormonal state. Therefore, the treatments suggested primarily involve at least a 5% weight loss, even in lean PCOS females, with a good response ultimately achieved. These treatments can include metformin and injectable weight loss or diabetes medications. The treatment plan is tailored around the individual health goals of each patient.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1930
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad classification that encompasses several distinct conditions. Within this category, مرض السكري دون مضاعفات refers to diabetes without complications, representing a form of the disease where hyperglycemia is present but secondary complications have not yet developed. فرط أنسولين الدم denotes hyperinsulinemia, a state of elevated insulin levels in the blood that can arise from pancreatic beta‑cell overactivity or insulin‑secreting tumors and may lead to hypoglycemia. ورم غلوكاغوني describes glucagonoma, a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, causing characteristic metabolic disturbances such as glucose intolerance, weight loss, and a distinctive skin rash. Together, these three entities illustrate the spectrum of disorders that fall under DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES, highlighting the varied clinical presentations and management considerations associated with pancreatic endocrine pathology.

agent2_snippets.content: Antithyroid preparations are pharmacologic agents that counteract excess thyroid hormone production and are employed in the treatment of hyperthyroidism (فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ). By inhibiting the synthesis of thyroid hormones, these medications help normalize elevated metabolic activity, alleviate symptoms such as tachycardia, weight loss, and tremor, and reduce the risk of complications associated with uncontrolled hyperthyroidism. Effective use of antithyroid preparations is therefore a central component of managing patients with فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ.

agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, اعتلال عصبي سكري, فرط أنسولين الدم, and ورم غلوكاغوني. ارتفاع السكر في الدم (high blood sugar) is a primary manifestation of diabetes, reflecting impaired glucose regulation by the endocrine pancreas. اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy) is a common chronic complication of prolonged hyperglycemia, affecting peripheral nerves and leading to sensory and motor deficits. فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia) denotes excessive insulin secretion, which can occur in early insulin resistance or insulin‑producing tumors and may precede or accompany diabetes. ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma) is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, often resulting in persistent hyperglycemia and weight loss. Recognizing how these conditions are interrelated within endocrine pancreatic disease supports comprehensive diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic strategies for affected patients.

Among the many obstetric complications encompassed by this larger category, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium represents a specific type of complication. This relationship underscores that gestational diabetes and related disorders of glucose metabolism are recognized as important pregnancy‑related complications and are included in the overall spectrum of conditions that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period. Effective identification and management of these glucose‑related complications are essential components of comprehensive obstetric care.
agent2_snippets.query_used:
A 27-year-old woman presents to the office with concerns about her long struggle with her physical appearance since adolescence. She says she has always been "large" and was constantly targeted by her classmates and coworkers for being so. Her main concern at the moment is her acne and unwanted facial hair on her upper lip, for which she often visits a local spa. She has tried numerous diet plans, exercise regimens, and cosmetic products with little to no effect. Recently, she underwent a glucose tolerance test that showed a plasma glucose level of 160 mg/dL (8.9 mmol/L) after 2 hours of a 75 g dose of oral glucose. She has a family history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and a menstrual cycle that occurs every 45 days. Her pulse is 72/min and the blood pressure is 138/80 mm Hg. On physical examination, her height is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) and her weight is 85 kg (187 lb), and she has severe inflammatory acne over the cheeks and forehead and dark coarse hairs on the back. What is the most likely diagnosis of this patient?

Options:
A: Hypothyroidism
B: Idiopathic hirsutism
C: Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS)
D: Ovarian hyperthecosis

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1875
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several related disorders: ارتفاع السكر في الدم (elevated blood glucose), مرض السكري دون مضاعفات (diabetes without complications), اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma). These entities represent a spectrum of pancreatic endocrine dysfunction, ranging from simple hyperglycemia to specific complications such as neuropathy, as well as hormonal excess states like hyperinsulinemia and glucagon‑secreting tumors. Understanding that all of these conditions are subtypes of diseases of the endocrine pancreas, including diabetes, helps clinicians recognize the varied presentations, anticipate potential complications, and tailor comprehensive patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
agent2_snippets.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1530
agent2_snippets.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are clinical manifestations of فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي (primary hypertension). An increase in blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم) is also associated with فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي. While فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي represents essential hypertension, it is conceptually linked to فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (secondary hypertension) as part of the broader hypertension classification, distinguishing primary from secondary causes. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians recognize the hemodynamic signs of primary hypertension and differentiate it from secondary forms for appropriate evaluation and management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
agent2_snippets.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
agent2_snippets.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.
